title: Gate load-use stalls by actual source operands
motivation: The current load-use hazard check compares the load destination against both raw rs1 and rs2 bitfields of the next instruction, even when that instruction does not architecturally read one or both fields. CoreMark contains many immediate, load, jump, and upper-immediate instructions where bits [24:20] or [19:15] are immediate or destination payload rather than a consumed register, so false positive hazards inject avoidable bubbles.

hypothesis.motivation: The current load-use hazard check compares the load destination against both raw rs1 and rs2 bitfields of the next instruction, even when that instruction does not architecturally read one or both fields. CoreMark contains many immediate, load, jump, and upper-immediate instructions where bits [24:20] or [19:15] are immediate or destination payload rather than a consumed register, so false positive hazards inject avoidable bubbles.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Make the load-use detector source-aware by deriving simple IF/ID source-use bits from the opcode and gating the rd==rs comparisons with those bits. Preserve conservative behavior for instruction classes that genuinely read both operands and for unknown/reserved encodings, but skip stalls for rs2 on OP-IMM/LOAD/JALR and for both sources on LUI/AUIPC/JAL/FENCE/EBREAK. This should reduce unnecessary one-cycle bubbles without changing the pipeline structure, RVFI retirement contract, or memory behavior, with only a small decode-side combinational cost.

title: Iterative DIV/REM execute sidecar
motivation: The baseline core keeps RV32M division and remainder in the combinational ALU, which likely consumes a large fraction of the LUT budget and contributes to the critical path. CoreMark's timed region is multiply-heavy but should rarely execute DIV/REM, so moving only DIV/REM off the single-cycle path can improve Fmax with little cycle-count cost.

hypothesis.motivation: The baseline core keeps RV32M division and remainder in the combinational ALU, which likely consumes a large fraction of the LUT budget and contributes to the critical path. CoreMark's timed region is multiply-heavy but should rarely execute DIV/REM, so moving only DIV/REM off the single-cycle path can improve Fmax with little cycle-count cost.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Restructure execute so ADD/SUB/logic/shift/compare/load-address/branch/MUL stay single-cycle, while DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU are handled by a small iterative divider sidecar. When a DIV/REM reaches EX, hold IF and ID, emit bubbles into EX/MEM while the divider runs, then retire the completed instruction normally through MEM/WB with unchanged RVFI ordering. This removes `/` and `%` from the hot combinational ALU path and should reduce LUT pressure and improve nextpnr Fmax without changing the top-level IO or the single-retire RVFI channel convention.

title: Elastic instruction fetch buffer
motivation: The current front end ties instruction availability directly to io_imemReady: any random iStall freezes the PC and injects a fetch bubble into decode. The CoreMark fitness run intentionally uses about 22% random instruction stalls, and the existing pipeline also has dmem/load-use/DIV hold cycles during which the instruction side could safely prefetch sequential fall-through instructions. The previous BTB attempt showed that adding combinational prediction lookup in IF can cost more Fmax than it saves, so the next structural front-end change should decouple stalls without a predictor table on the fetch critical path.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Replace the PC-only IF stage with a tiny fall-through instruction fetch buffer, using a 2-entry FIFO plus same-cycle bypass when the FIFO is empty and io_imemReady is high. When decode/EX/MEM are stalled, IF continues issuing sequential fetch addresses and enqueues returned instructions until the FIFO is full; when io_imemReady drops later, decode can drain buffered instructions instead of immediately bubbling. On any branch/jump redirect, flush the FIFO and restart at the redirect target, preserving the current resolve-in-EX control flow and single-retire RVFI ordering. This should recover a portion of the random iStall CPI loss with modest registers/control logic and little Fmax risk because there is no BTB tag/counter/target lookup in the IF address path.

title: Static backward branch predictor
motivation:
The previous dynamic BTB predictor regressed because it added tag, counter,
and target lookup logic directly to the fetch path. The core still pays a
redirect bubble for every taken direct control transfer, but CoreMark's hot
loops do not necessarily need a dynamic table to recover that CPI.

hypothesis.motivation:

redirect bubble for every taken direct control transfer, but CoreMark's hot
loops do not necessarily need a dynamic table to recover that CPI.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the table-based approach with a stateless static predictor in IF:
predict direct JAL as taken, predict conditional branches with negative B
immediates as taken, and otherwise fetch fall-through. Carry the predicted
next PC through IF/ID and ID/EX, then have EX redirect only when the resolved
next PC differs from the prediction. This should recover most hot backward-loop
bubbles with only immediate decode plus an alternate PC adder, avoiding the
BTB storage/tag critical-path cost that hurt the prior predictor attempt. Gate
predicted targets to aligned in-range instruction addresses so wrong-path
speculative fetches do not violate the memory-bound contract.

title: Late store-data forwarding
motivation: The current load-use detector stalls whenever a load destination matches either raw source field of the following instruction. That is necessary when the next instruction needs the loaded value in EX, but a store only needs rs2 data when it reaches MEM. CoreMark has copy/update patterns where a loaded word or halfword is stored shortly afterward, so treating load-to-store-data as a normal load-use hazard can add avoidable bubbles.

title: Zero-bubble load-use forwarding
motivation: The current pipeline always inserts a one-cycle bubble when a LOAD in ID/EX is followed by an instruction whose raw source fields match the load destination. That is safe, but it leaves CPI on the table in the common zero-wait or ready dmem case: by the time the dependent instruction reaches EX, the older load is in MEM and the formatted load result is available combinationally. CoreMark's 6 KB working set has many pointer and scalar load-use chains, while the prior source-use experiment showed that any optimization here must preserve exact post-forward RVFI source data rather than merely skipping comparisons.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Restructure the load-use path so a dependent instruction can enter EX immediately behind a load and receive the current MEM-stage load result through the existing operand forwarding muxes. The MEM stage should expose the same sign/zero-extended load_data it will write into MEM/WB, forward_unit/core should select that value instead of EX/MEM.alu_result for load producers, and hazard_unit should stop injecting an unconditional load-use bubble. This keeps retirement in order, records the forwarded rs1/rs2 values into RVFI, and trades a small forwarding mux addition for fewer CoreMark load-use bubbles without adding front-end predictor or FIFO logic.

motivation:

title: Shared branch compare flags
motivation:

MEM/WB-to-EX forwarding currently routes through the combinational writeback mux (`mem_to_reg ? read_data : alu_result`) before reaching the EX operand muxes. That mux is unnecessary after the MEM/WB flop because the selected writeback value is already known when MEM clocks the register.

Move the `mem_to_reg` writeback-data selection into `mem_stage` when the MEM/WB register is written, and carry a single final `wb_data` payload forward instead of separate `alu_result` and `read_data` writeback sources. `wb_stage` then becomes a direct pass-through of the precomputed value, and `core.sv` continues to use that same value for MEM/WB forwarding and RVFI `rd_wdata`.

This should remove one 32-bit mux from the registered MEM/WB-to-EX forwarding path and trim one now-dead 32-bit MEM/WB payload, without changing instruction timing, dmem behavior, load formatting, store-buffer ordering, RVFI order, trap reporting, or the two-channel top-level port contract. The added mux moves to the MEM-stage register input, where the design already computes `load_data` and captures pipeline state, making this a lower-risk timing trade than new predictor or store-buffer control.

title: EX-precomputed memory lanes
motivation: The current best core already won most of its fitness from structural changes that avoided CPI cost on the hot path: DIV/REM moved out of EX, zero-wait load-use chains forward directly from MEM, and a one-entry store buffer hides some random dStall. title: Redirect-boundary target instruction memo
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts repeatedly lost more Fmax than they recovered in CPI because they put target lookup, immediate decode, exact-PC compare, or predicted-next-PC metadata on the steady-state fetch path. The remaining predictor opportunity is to avoid predicting before decode entirely and instead remove the bubble after an already-resolved taken redirect.

CoreMark hot loops repeatedly redirect to the same first target instruction. A tiny branch-target instruction memo can reuse that first target instruction on later redirects without changing the normal fall-through fetch path, top-level IO, RVFI ordering, or channel-1 tie-off.

immediate generation, and ID/EX control setup in the same cycle.

title: Backend-stall fetch replay latch
motivation:
The current IF stage holds the PC during MEM stalls and DIV holds, but any
instruction word that arrives from imem during that backend stall is discarded
because ID cannot consume it. If the backend releases on a cycle where the
random CoreMark iStall model drops io_imemReady, decode still receives a NOP
bubble even though the held-PC instruction was visible earlier. Prior fetch
FIFO and registered IF/ID attempts regressed because they changed the normal
fetch/decode path, so the safer structural opportunity is to exploit only the
already-stalled backend window.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry replay latch in IF that fills only while a backend stall holds
the PC and io_imemReady supplies the instruction for that held PC. When the
backend can advance but io_imemReady is low, IF emits the latched instruction
and PC as a valid IF/ID payload for one cycle, advances the PC normally, and
clears the latch. Normal no-stall fetch remains the existing combinational
io_imemData-to-decode path; reset and any redirect invalidate the latch.

The hazard unit should treat `io_imemReady || replay_valid` as instruction
availability, so a replayed instruction is not flushed as an iStall bubble.
This should recover cycles at intersections of dmem/DIV backend stalls and
random iStall with only one 32-bit instruction register, a valid bit, and a
small IF output mux, avoiding predictor state, FIFO full/empty control, or
target arithmetic on the fragile fetch path.

title: Registered lookahead target predictor
motivation:
The prior predictor attempts mostly failed because the current fetch path is fragile: same-cycle BTB reads, immediate decode, exact-PC compares, target adders, and full predicted-next-PC metadata all cost more Fmax than CoreMark recovered in CPI. The remaining predictor opportunity is to keep the current PC update path as close to fall-through as possible while still learning repeated loop backedges.

CoreMark still pays recurring taken-branch redirects and also runs with random instruction stalls, so a very small predictor can help if its lookup work is moved off the current-cycle next-PC decision and if EX remains fully authoritative for every repair.

While fetching PC N, the predictor compares the already-computed fall-through PC N+4 against the saved source PC and registers only a hit bit plus target for the next cycle. title: Shared ALU barrel shifter
motivation:
The current best core has already captured the large gains from removing DIV/REM from the hot ALU path, forwarding ready loads, buffering normal-RAM stores, and replaying fetches after backend stalls. Recent micro-opts regressed when they added control plumbing or tried to outguess synthesis on branch, reset, memory-lane, and decode cones.

A remaining local datapath target is the ALU variable-shift logic. rtl/alu.sv expresses SLL, SRL, and SRA as three independent shifts in the same result case. On a LUT FPGA, separate barrel-shift mux trees can consume routing and output-mux depth even though only one shift result is ever selected.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the three ALU shift expressions with one shared directional barrel shifter. Use bit-reversal wiring around a single right-shift network for SLL, use zero-fill for SRL, and use sign-fill for SRA; ADD/SUB/logic/compare/LUI/MUL and the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions stay otherwise unchanged.

This is a pure local micro-optimization: no new pipeline state, no CPI change, no predictor or memory-control plumbing, and no top-level IO or RVFI contract change. If Yosys/nextpnr currently builds separate shift mux trees, sharing them should trim ALU LUT/routing pressure and may improve Fmax without repeating the failed broad rewrites from prior rounds.

title: Backend-stall two-word fetch replay
motivation: The latest structural win came from a one-entry IF replay latch that fills only while MEM or DIV holds the backend, preserving the normal combinational fetch-to-decode path. During longer backend stalls, however, the instruction port can often deliver more than the single held-PC word, and the current design discards that opportunity by keeping io_imemAddr fixed at the stalled PC. Prior fetch FIFOs and registered IF/ID slots regressed because they affected the always-active front-end path, so the next structural step should stay confined to already-stalled cycles.

Extend rtl/if_stage.sv from a one-word backend-stall replay latch into a tiny two-word sequential replay window that is active only while backend_stall holds ID. First capture the held-PC instruction exactly as today; after that slot is valid and the backend is still stalled, temporarily drive io_imemAddr to PC+4 and capture a second fall-through word when io_imemReady is high. When the backend releases under an instruction-side stall, replay the cached words in order for the current PC and next PC, advancing the real PC normally as each word is consumed. Normal no-stall fetch remains the existing io_imemData-to-decode path, and reset or any EX redirect invalidates both replay slots.

This should recover additional random iStall bubbles after dmem stalls and rare multi-cycle DIV holds without introducing predictor state, FIFO full/empty logic, decode injection, or same-cycle target math on the hot path. The implementation must keep the single-retire RVFI convention unchanged and ensure stale fall-through replay data cannot survive redirects.

motivation:
Prior predictor attempts repeatedly regressed because they put tag compares,
immediate decode, target adders, or predicted-PC metadata on the normal fetch
path. The current best core has one front-end mechanism that already helped:
a replay latch filled only while backend stalls hold the PC. That stalled

title: Predecode divider sidecar bit
motivation:
The current core already has the large wins from the DIV/REM sidecar, ready-load forwarding, store buffering, and backend-stall fetch replay. Recent micro-opts regressed when they added muxing to hot ALU, branch, memory-lane, reset, or decoder paths, so the next attempt should be a very small cleanup that removes existing EX control logic rather than adding a new datapath.

EX currently redetects DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU with a four-way compare on the 5-bit ALU opcode to drive the sidecar busy/start path. At the same time, the ID/EX control bundle carries `is_lui`, but LUI execution is already fully represented by `alu_op = ALU_LUI` and `alu_src = 1`; the pipelined `is_lui` bit is not consumed after decode.

title: PC-tagged backend replay window
motivation:
The current best structural change is the one-entry backend-stall IF replay latch, which improved fitness by preserving an instruction word that arrived while MEM or DIV held the backend. Longer backend stalls can also expose the next fall-through word, but the previous two-word replay attempt broke because an untagged second word could survive past redirects or mismatched PC consumption and later feed stale instructions into decode.

A corrected replay window should keep the normal fetch-to-decode path data-only and unchanged for no-stall cycles, while safely recovering more random iStall release bubbles after backend holds.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extend the existing IF replay latch into a two-entry PC-tagged backend replay window. Slot 0 captures the held-PC instruction exactly as today, but stores its PC tag; only after slot 0 is valid and the backend is still stalled may IF temporarily drive the instruction bus to PC+4 and capture slot 1 with a PC+4 tag. When the backend releases under an instruction-side stall, IF may emit a replayed instruction only if a valid slot tag exactly matches the current PC, consuming matching slots in order as the PC advances.

Reset, any EX redirect, any tag mismatch, and normal live fetch consumption should invalidate stale entries so the stale fall-through bug from the prior attempt cannot recur. Because the second fetch address and extra slot controls are active only while the backend is already stalled, the common io_imemData-to-decode path remains close to the proven one-entry replay design, but CoreMark can recover additional cycles after multi-cycle dmem stalls and DIV holds.

changes.description: Replace the single replay latch with a two-entry PC-tagged replay window that fills only during backend stalls, fetches PC+4 only after the held-PC slot is valid, and replays entries only on exact current-PC tag matches.
Keep the hazard unit's instruction-available input driven by live imem readiness or any exact-match replay availability from IF, without changing the top-level IO or RVFI retirement contract.
Add a regression covering backend-stall replay across redirects and PC mismatches so stale fall-through entries cannot be consumed after the pipe resumes.

title: Redirect-target replay capture
motivation: Prior predictor attempts for this core consistently regressed when they added target lookup, immediate decode, exact-PC compares, predicted-next-PC metadata, or redirect-suppression control to the normal fetch path. The one front-end idea that improved fitness was data-only IF replay filled during backend stalls, because it recovered random iStall bubbles without changing the common io_imemData-to-decode path. Taken branches and jumps still create a vulnerable redirect boundary: if the target instruction is visible during the redirect cycle but io_imemReady drops on the next cycle, the pipeline injects another fetch bubble.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an opportunistic redirect-target replay predictor in IF. On an authoritative EX redirect, drive the instruction address to redirect_target during the flush cycle and, when io_imemReady is high, capture that target PC and instruction in a one-entry replay slot. On the following cycle, if the real PC matches the captured target and io_imemReady is low, emit the captured instruction as the IF/ID payload and let the PC advance normally.

This is deliberately data-only: it does not predict branch direction, does not suppress EX redirects, does not carry predicted-next-PC metadata through the pipeline, and invalidates on reset or any newer redirect. It should recover some CoreMark cycles at the intersection of taken redirects and the 22% random instruction-stall model while adding only a small redirect-cycle address mux and one PC-tagged replay entry off the normal fall-through path.
